facts.text: Fukuzawa Yukichi wrote an autobiography.
The autobiography of Fukuzawa Yukichi was published and is available as a full text document.
Fukuzawa Yukichi taught Japanese people about world history and geography when they were unfamiliar with modern knowledge.
Fukuzawa Yukichi taught elementary principles of physics, chemistry, and astronomy.
Fukuzawa Yukichi taught methods in bookkeeping by double entry.
Fukuzawa Yukichi taught the art of public speaking.
Fukuzawa Yukichi taught the science of military tactics in field warfare and siege of fortresses.
Fukuzawa Yukichi taught the construction and handling of infantry rifles.
General Murata obtained his first knowledge on rifles and the art of shooting from Fukuzawa's book.
Fukuzawa Yukichi called himself an unofficial teacher and adviser of the Meiji government.
Fukuzawa Yukichi died in 1901.
Fukuzawa Yukichi lived to see the results of his efforts to modernize Japan.
Fukuzawa Yukichi wrote an autobiography shortly after the Sino-Japanese war of 1894-1895.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was a man of many feelings and many tears.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was reared on Chinese philosophy in his youth.
Fukuzawa Yukichi did not hesitate to break through restrictive Oriental philosophy.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father was Fukuzawa Hyakusuke, a samurai of the Okudaira Clan of Nakatsu.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother was O-Jun, daughter of Hashimoto Hamaemon.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father was a samurai of lower rank, barely high enough to have formal audience with the lord.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father was made overseer of the treasury.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father spent much time at his lord's storage office and headquarters in Osaka.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was born on December 12 in the fifth year of the Tempo era (January 10, 1835).
Fukuzawa Yukichi was the youngest of five children: one older brother and three older sisters.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father was forty-three years old when Fukuzawa was born.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother was thirty-one years old when Fukuzawa was born.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father died a year and a half after his birth.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's brother was eleven years old when their father died.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother took her children back to Nakatsu after her husband's death.
Fukuzawa Yukichi and his siblings did not mix well with other children in Nakatsu society.
Fukuzawa Yukichi had dozens of cousins in Nakatsu.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was the youngest of five children.
Fukuzawa Yukichi had one older brother and three older sisters.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father died when Fukuzawa was a year and a half old.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's family moved to Nakatsu, the original feudal province of his mother.
Fukuzawa Yukichi and his siblings did not mix well with other children in Nakatsu.
Fukuzawa Yukichi and his siblings spoke an Osaka dialect, which made them self-conscious in Nakatsu.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother had become accustomed to life in Osaka, the most prosperous city in Japan.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father was a scholar.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father disdained to spend thought on money or touch it.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father longed for a quiet scholarly life with books and ancient philosophy.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father worked as a treasury overseer.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father had to associate with merchants and negotiate with rich men like Kajimaya and Konoike of Osaka.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father was unhappy in his worldly affairs.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father removed his children from a teacher who taught them numerals.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father believed it was abominable to teach children to use numbers, the tool of merchants.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father was a strict father who never compromised on what he felt was right.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father was a Confucian to the very heart.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father had particular respect for Ito Togai among great scholars on Chinese philosophy.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father lived by the saying 'Be true and sincere, unashamed even in the innermost privacy.'
Fukuzawa Yukichi and his siblings lived with few friends to visit them.
Fukuzawa Yukichi and his siblings were influenced only by their mother, who lived in the memory of their father.
Fukuzawa Yukichi and his siblings unconsciously formed a group apart in Nakatsu.
Fukuzawa Yukichi and his siblings looked upon their neighbors as less refined than themselves.
Fukuzawa Yukichi and his siblings felt their cousins were not quite like themselves.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was a lively, happy child fond of talk and romping about.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was never good at climbing trees.
Fukuzawa Yukichi never learned to swim.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's home life was a happy one despite living apart in an alien place.
Fukuzawa Yukichi and his siblings never quarreled among themselves or annoyed their mother.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother was not strict and did not take particular pains in teaching manners.
Fukuzawa Yukichi and his siblings grew up naturally to be obedient and thoughtful.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's family never had a musical instrument like shamisen in their home.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's family never thought of hearing shamisen because it was an amusement unworthy of the samurai.
Fukuzawa Yukichi never thought of going to see a play.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother never let herself mention the theater.
Fukuzawa Yukichi and his siblings never asked questions about plays.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's family was perhaps the only samurai family of middle and low class in Nakatsu that did not see plays.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father submitted to his distasteful position and small stipend.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father buried his discontent in his heart.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was found to be a rather thin but big-boned child at birth.
A midwife said Fukuzawa Yukichi would grow up to be a fine man if fed plenty of milk.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father was glad about the midwife's assessment.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father wanted to make him a priest when he reached ten or eleven years old.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father wanted to send him to a monastery.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother never understood why his father wanted him to be a priest.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother said that if his father were still living, he would be a priest of some temple.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father's wish for him to become a priest was a result of the feudal system's rigid law of inheritance.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father saw the priesthood as the only possible road to advancement for his son.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father lived forty-five years of his life in the fetters of the feudal system.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father died before any of his desires had been fulfilled.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father determined to put his son in a monastery so he might have a wider field of thought and life.
Fukuzawa Yukichi considers the feudal system to be his father's mortal enemy.
Fukuzawa Yukichi is honor-bound to destroy the feudal system.
Fukuzawa Yukichi did not become a priest despite his father's wish.
Fukuzawa Yukichi did not do any studying at home as his father would have encouraged.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's brother took his father's place in the family.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's brother was still a young man after their father's death.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother was obliged to do all the housework.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother fed and clothed five children by herself.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother did not have enough means to hire a servant.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's education was neglected in the busy rush of daily work.
Young sons of the Nakatsu clan studied Chinese classics such as Lun-yu and Ta-hsiieh.
Fukuzawa Yukichi professed a dislike of books in his youth.
Fukuzawa Yukichi became ashamed of himself at fourteen or fifteen years old.
Fukuzawa Yukichi willingly started to school at fourteen or fifteen years old.
Fukuzawa Yukichi found that many boys of his age were studying Chinese classics.
Fukuzawa Yukichi began studying with the oral reading of Mencius at fifteen years old.
Other boys of Fukuzawa Yukichi's age were discussing the books of Chinese philosophy when he started.
Advanced students gave lessons in oral reading to new students early in the morning at Fukuzawa Yukichi's school.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was somewhat talented in literature.
Fukuzawa Yukichi could discuss a book with the older student who taught him.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was always upsetting the argument of his older student teacher.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's older student teacher knew the words well but was slow to take in the ideas they expressed.
Fukuzawa Yukichi changed school two or three times.
Fukuzawa Yukichi studied most under the care of a master named Shiraishi.
Fukuzawa Yukichi made rapid progress under Shiraishi's guidance.
Fukuzawa Yukichi had no difficulty studying a good part of the Chinese classics in four or five years.
Shiraishi Sensei placed special emphasis on the classics.
Fukuzawa Yukichi and his classmates gave much time to the study of Lun-yu and Mencius.
Shiraishi Sensei was fond of Shih-ching and Shu-ching.
Fukuzawa Yukichi often listened to Shiraishi's lectures on Shih-ching and Shu-ching.
Fukuzawa Yukichi read Tso-chuan eleven times over.
Most of Fukuzawa Yukichi's fellow students gave up Tso-chuan after reading three or four volumes out of fifteen.
Fukuzawa Yukichi read all fifteen volumes of Tso-chuan.
Fukuzawa Yukichi memorized the most interesting passages of Tso-chuan.
Fukuzawa Yukichi became zenza, or senior disciple, at Shiraishi's school.
Fukuzawa Yukichi had the privilege of giving occasional lectures as a senior disciple.
Shiraishi Sensei belonged to the school of Kamei.
Shiraishi Sensei worshipped Kamei as a master of sound philosophy.
Shiraishi Sensei rather despised the delicately literary.
Shiraishi Sensei did not encourage the writing of lyric poetry among his students.
Shiraishi Sensei disparagingly spoke of poet and satirist Hirose Tanso.
Shiraishi Sensei said Hirose Tanso could not write a line of perfect Chinese.
Shiraishi Sensei said Hirose Tanso was but a trifling poet in Japanese.
Shiraishi Sensei disparagingly spoke of literary contemporary Rai Sanyo.
Shiraishi Sensei said if Rai Sanyo's writings are called 'literature,' then anybody's scribblings might be literature too.
Fukuzawa Yukichi and his fellow disciples learned to think little of those their master denounced.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father was like Shiraishi in his views.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father and Rai Sanyo never exchanged courtesies despite living in nearby cities.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's father became a friend of scholar Noda Tekiho.
Fukuzawa Yukichi does not know what kind of man Noda Tekiho was.
Kamei Sensei established his own theory in opposition to the Chu-tzu School.
Kamei Sensei's disciples were often at odds with scholars of other groups.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was very clever at doing little things with his hands.
Fukuzawa Yukichi loved to try inventing and devising things.
Fukuzawa Yukichi contrived means to fish things out of the well.
Fukuzawa Yukichi bent nails in many ways to open a failed drawer lock.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was good at pasting new paper on the inner doors of the house called shoji.
Fukuzawa Yukichi did the work of pasting new paper on shoji for his own house.
Fukuzawa Yukichi sometimes was hired out by relatives to help with shoji work in their houses.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was quick and clever at little jobs of every kind.
Fukuzawa Yukichi mended wooden clogs and sandals for all his family.
Fukuzawa Yukichi fixed broken doors and leaks in the roof.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's family was poor.
Fukuzawa Yukichi bought a large needle and changed the covering of the tatami.
Fukuzawa Yukichi knew how to split bamboo and put hoops around buckets and tubs.
Fukuzawa Yukichi began to earn money by making wooden clogs.
Fukuzawa Yukichi began to earn money by fitting out swords.
Fukuzawa Yukichi never learned to polish the blade of a sword.
Fukuzawa Yukichi could lacquer the sheath of a sword.
Fukuzawa Yukichi could wind the cords around the handle of a sword.
Fukuzawa Yukichi could put on the metal fittings of a sword.
Fukuzawa Yukichi still has a short sword which he fitted out himself.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's short sword is of poor workmanship.
Fukuzawa Yukichi learned sword-fitting arts from various acquaintances among the samurai.
Fukuzawa Yukichi learned metalwork from samurai who practiced these arts to add to their living.
Fukuzawa Yukichi had a difficult time making a good file for metalwork.
Fukuzawa Yukichi knew how to make an ordinary file from a steel bar after a fashion.
Fukuzawa Yukichi could not make a fine file for sharpening saws.
Fukuzawa Yukichi came to Yedo years later.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was surprised at the sight of a blacksmith's apprentice making a saw-file in Yedo.
The blacksmith's apprentice Fukuzawa Yukichi saw was at Tamachi on the right-hand side of the street as he entered the city.
The blacksmith's apprentice had the file on a piece of leather on an anvil.
Fukuzawa Yukichi learned to lacquer sword sheaths, wind cords around handles, and fit metal fittings.
Fukuzawa Yukichi still possessed a short sword that he fitted out himself in his childhood.
Fukuzawa Yukichi learned sword-fitting arts from various samurai acquaintances.
Fukuzawa Yukichi knew how to make an ordinary file from a steel bar.
Fukuzawa Yukichi came to Yedo and was surprised to see a blacksmith's apprentice making a saw-file.
Fukuzawa Yukichi observed the apprentice making a saw-file at Tamachi on the right-hand side of the street.
Fukuzawa Yukichi has been accustomed to working with his hands since childhood.
Fukuzawa Yukichi continued to handle planes and chisels in his old age.
Fukuzawa Yukichi possessed little of what people call 'good taste.'
Fukuzawa Yukichi cared nothing for the kind of clothes he wore or the kind of house he lived in.
Fukuzawa Yukichi did not understand why one garment must be worn over another in a certain way.
Fukuzawa Yukichi did not understand why fashions in dress should change every year.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's one taste was the sword.
Fukuzawa Yukichi knew what constituted good workmanship in sword fitting.
Fukuzawa Yukichi believed his taste for swords came from his early work in sword fitting.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's skill in sword fitting was never more than that of an amateur.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was always unconcerned with the way of society.
Fukuzawa Yukichi had an inborn nature to act always in his own way.
Fukuzawa Yukichi hated having a towel on his face.
Fukuzawa Yukichi never wore a face-wrapping towel.
Fukuzawa Yukichi went out on errands in broad daylight swinging a wine bottle in one hand.
Fukuzawa Yukichi carried two swords at his side as became a man of samurai rank.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was fond of wine.
Fukuzawa Yukichi often cooked burdocks and radishes to help his mother in the kitchen when guests were expected.
Fukuzawa Yukichi disappeared into a closet when guests arrived at his house.
Fukuzawa Yukichi did not like to see guests lazily eating and drinking and talking nonsense.
Fukuzawa Yukichi often wished guests would hurry and go away.
Fukuzawa Yukichi would take his supper early and crawl into a closet where bedding was kept.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's brother had many friends who used to come in the evening and discuss questions of the day.
Fukuzawa Yukichi sometimes listened to his brother's friends but was never allowed to join in as a youngster.
Fukuzawa Yukichi heard conversations about Rekko of Mito and Shungaku of Echizen.
Fukuzawa Yukichi believed that Rekko of Mito was the greatest man in the world.
Fukuzawa Yukichi heard that Egawa Tarozaemon was a hero of modern times for his self-control in living through winters in summer clothing.
Fukuzawa Yukichi began to sleep on the floor rolled up in only one quilt without disclosing his intention to anybody.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother was worried when she learned he was sleeping in only one quilt.
Fukuzawa Yukichi endured the cold until spring when he was fifteen or sixteen years old.
Fukuzawa Yukichi had a strong constitution.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's brother was a thorough scholar in Chinese.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's brother studied mathematics according to the teaching of Hoashi Banri.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's brother believed in the moral teachings of the Chinese to the core.
Fukuzawa Yukichi told his brother he would like to be the richest man in Japan and spend all the money he wanted.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's brother answered that he would be dutiful to his parents, faithful to his brethren, and loyal to his master until death.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's brother said he was born the eldest son and was the head of the family.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's brother expressed a desire to become an adopted son of a very difficult family with headstrong parents.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's brother believed all troubles between parents and an adopted son came from the wilfulness of the son.
Fukuzawa Yukichi had an entirely opposite opinion about being an adopted son.
Fukuzawa Yukichi said he would hate to be an adopted son.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was sixteen or seventeen years old when he had the conversation with his brother about adoption.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother was an unusual woman who thought individually on certain matters.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother's family belonged to the Shin sect of Buddhism.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother would never go to hear a sermon as was expected of everyone in the Shin sect.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother would not worship Amida Buddha because she felt shy in worshipping before Amida Sama.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother never missed paying respects to the graves of her husband and ancestors on a certain day in each month.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother took a bagful of rice to the temple regularly.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother had many friends among priests.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother loved to treat novices with tasty dishes when they came to visit.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother was fond of doing kindnesses to all people.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother made friends among the classes beneath her own, the merchants and farmers.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother had no objection to admitting beggars.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother had no objection to admitting the outcast eta.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother never showed any sign of slighting lower classes.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother spoke respectfully to people of lower classes.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother called a half-witted beggar woman named Chie into her house.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother made the beggar woman sit on the grass in the yard.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother tied her own sleeves behind her back and bared her arms to catch vermin from the beggar's hair.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was called on to help his mother catch vermin from the beggar woman's hair.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was ordered to stand by with a stone to crush the vermin his mother pulled from the beggar's hair.
Fukuzawa Yukichi hated helping his mother catch vermin from the beggar woman's hair.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mother gave the beggar woman a bowl of rice after catching vermin from her hair.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was twelve or thirteen years old when he ran through the room and stepped on papers his brother was arranging.
Fukuzawa Yukichi stepped on papers bearing his lord's name, Okudaira Taizen-no Tayo.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's brother scolded him for stepping on their lord's name.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's brother began to recite the samurai rules of duty after scolding him.
Fukuzawa Yukichi apologized to his brother but felt no genuine apology in his heart.
Fukuzawa Yukichi reasoned in his childish mind that stepping on a piece of paper was not wrong.
Fukuzawa Yukichi stole a sacred charm bearing sacred names and deliberately trampled on it.
Fukuzawa Yukichi experienced no heavenly vengeance after trampling on a sacred charm.
Fukuzawa Yukichi took a sacred charm to the privy and put it in the filth.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was a little afraid after desecrating the sacred charm in the privy.
Fukuzawa Yukichi experienced no punishment after desecrating the sacred charm in the privy.
Fukuzawa Yukichi felt he had made a great discovery about the falseness of sacred objects.
Fukuzawa Yukichi could not tell anybody about his discovery regarding sacred objects.
Fukuzawa Yukichi became more reckless as he grew older.
Fukuzawa Yukichi decided that talk about divine punishment was a lie used by old men to scold children.
Fukuzawa Yukichi conceived the idea of finding out what the god of Inari really was.
Fukuzawa Yukichi opened an Inari shrine in the corner of his uncle's garden.
Fukuzawa Yukichi found only a stone in the Inari shrine.
Fukuzawa Yukichi threw away the stone and put in another stone he picked up on the road.
Fukuzawa Yukichi explored the Inari shrine of his neighbor, Shimomura.
Fukuzawa Yukichi found a wooden tablet as the token of the god in Shimomura's Inari shrine.
Fukuzawa Yukichi threw away the wooden tablet from Shimomura's shrine.
Fukuzawa Yukichi waited to see what might happen after desecrating the shrines.
Fukuzawa Yukichi chuckled to himself during the Inari festival when people worshipped his stones.
Fukuzawa Yukichi called the people worshipping his stones 'fools.'
Fukuzawa Yukichi has never had any fear of gods or Buddha since childhood.
Fukuzawa Yukichi has never had any faith in augury and magic.
Fukuzawa Yukichi has never had any faith in the fox and badger which people say have power to deceive men.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was a happy child.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's mind was never clouded by unreasonable fears.
A queer woman came to Fukuzawa Yukichi's town from Osaka.
The woman from Osaka was about thirty years old.
The woman from Osaka was a daughter of Dempojiya Matsuemon, the chief of the stevedores.
The woman from Osaka claimed she knew the magic of Inari.
Fukuzawa Yukichi stepped forward and said he would like to hold the ceremonial wand.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was fifteen or sixteen years old when the woman from Osaka came to his house.
Fukuzawa Yukichi said it would be fun to see what it feels like to have Inari Sama inside him.
The woman from Osaka looked at Fukuzawa Yukichi scrutinizingly and shook her head.
The woman from Osaka said Fukuzawa Yukichi would not do for the magic.
Fukuzawa Yukichi insisted the woman try the magic on him.
Fukuzawa Yukichi had a good time teasing the woman from Osaka.
The thing that made Fukuzawa Yukichi most unhappy in Nakatsu was the restriction of rank and position.
Distinctions between high and low were clearly defined in Nakatsu, not only on official occasions but in private intercourse and even among children.
Children of lower samurai families like Fukuzawa Yukichi's were obliged to use a respectful manner of address to children of high samurai families.
Children of high samurai families used an arrogant form of address to children of lower samurai families.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was the best student in school.
No children made light of Fukuzawa Yukichi in school.
Children of high samurai families gave themselves airs as superior to Fukuzawa Yukichi outside of school.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was sure he was no inferior to high samurai children, not even in physical power.
Fukuzawa Yukichi could not free himself from discontent about rank distinctions though he was still a child.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's brother sent a letter to the chancellor of the lord.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's brother addressed the outside cover of the letter in the scholarly style as 'Sama Kashitsuji.'
The letter came back with an order to change the address to 'Sama O-toritsugi Shu,' a much humbler mode of address.
Fukuzawa was from a lower samurai family.
Children of lower samurai families were obliged to use respectful forms of address when speaking to children of high samurai families.
Children of high samurai families used arrogant forms of address to children of lower samurai families.
Fukuzawa was the best student in his school.
Fukuzawa felt discontent about the feudal rank distinctions even as a child.
Fukuzawa's brother was reprimanded for using a scholarly style of address on a letter to the lord.
Fukuzawa determined to run away from Nakatsu after witnessing his brother's reprimand.
Fukuzawa's cousins were samurai of low rank who complained about the despotic atmosphere of the clan.
Fukuzawa advised his cousins not to complain about Nakatsu while staying there.
Fukuzawa read a Chinese book containing the words 'Never show joy or anger in the face.'
Fukuzawa adopted the philosophy of never showing joy or anger in his face.
Fukuzawa trained himself to receive both applause and disparagement politely.
Fukuzawa claims he was never truly angry in his life until one incident.
Fukuzawa never allowed his hands to touch a person in anger from his youth until an incident about twenty years into adulthood.
Fukuzawa had a pupil who was hopelessly dissipated despite his assistance.
Fukuzawa ordered his dissolute pupil to sit up all night and reflect on his actions.
Fukuzawa shook his pupil in anger when he found him snoring instead of reflecting.
Fukuzawa felt remorse after shaking his pupil in anger, comparing it to a priest breaking commandments.
Fukuzawa was fond of talking more than the average person.
Fukuzawa liked to be quick and active in everything he did.
Fukuzawa never indulged in heated boyish arguments.
Fukuzawa would evade his opponent's point when they grew heated in discussion.
Fukuzawa was always praying for an opportunity to leave Nakatsu.
Fukuzawa was willing to go anywhere and endure any hardship to leave Nakatsu.
Fukuzawa set out to Nagasaki in February of the first year of Ansei (1854).
Fukuzawa counted himself twenty-one years old when he went to Nagasaki, though his exact age was nineteen years and three months.
No one in Fukuzawa's town could understand Dutch letters at the time he left for Nagasaki.
Fukuzawa's departure to Nagasaki occurred a few months after Commodore Perry's arrival.
Fukuzawa's brother told him that anyone wanting to learn Western gunnery must study gensho.
Fukuzawa had no trouble learning Chinese, which gave him confidence in learning other languages.
Fukuzawa answered that he would study Dutch or any other language if others could learn it.
It took Fukuzawa three full days to learn the twenty-six letters of the alphabet.
Fukuzawa's true reason for going to Nagasaki was to get away from Nakatsu.
Fukuzawa swore to himself that he would never come back to Nakatsu, like a bullet shot from a gun's muzzle.
Fukuzawa spat on the ground and walked quickly away when leaving Nakatsu.
Fukuzawa first lived as a dependant in Koei-ji, a Buddhist temple in Nagasaki.
Okudaira Iki took Fukuzawa to the Buddhist temple in Nagasaki.
Yamamoto Monojiro took Fukuzawa as an 'eating guest' in his house.
Fukuzawa did all sorts of work in Yamamoto's household despite being supposed to be a free boarder.
Yamamoto had poor eyesight, and Fukuzawa read to him essays of contemporary scholars.
Fukuzawa gave lessons to Yamamoto's son, a youth of eighteen or nineteen.
Yamamoto was a poor man but lived extravagantly with many friends and followers.
Yamamoto had gone heavily into debt.
Fukuzawa negotiated postponement of Yamamoto's debts and contracted new debts.
Fukuzawa drew water from the well and swept the house in the mornings.
Fukuzawa washed his master's back in his bath.
Yamamoto's wife kept many cats and lap dogs in the house and bigger dogs in the yard.
Fukuzawa took care of Yamamoto's animals.
Yamamoto asked Fukuzawa to become his adopted son.
Fukuzawa had been promised to his uncle Nakamura's family as his heir since childhood.
Yamamoto said he would do all in his power to look after Fukuzawa's future if he joined his family.
Yamamoto had a collection of hand-written books as his private property.
Yamamoto's income came partly from charges on lending books or selling handwritten copies.
Fukuzawa was given charge of Yamamoto's book lending and copying business.
Nagasaki was the only part of Japan in contact with the outside world through the Dutch compound.
Students of gunnery and foreign affairs came to Nagasaki from many different clans.
Yamamoto could arrange visits to the Dutch compound on the island of Dejima.
Yamamoto could furnish diagrams and directions for casting cannon.
Fukuzawa was a mere amateur in gunnery and had never seen a gun in operation.
Fukuzawa drew diagrams and wrote directions for gunnery despite being an amateur.
Okudaira Iki and Fukuzawa exchanged places, with Fukuzawa coming to occupy a position in gunnery while Iki remained a student.
The exchange of positions caused a break between Fukuzawa and Iki.
Fukuzawa's chief concern was learning the Dutch language.
Fukuzawa went to the interpreter's house and the house of special physicians who practiced Dutch medicine.
After fifty or a hundred days, Fukuzawa came to understand something of the Dutch language.
Okudaira Iki never really learned Dutch.
Iki was a spoiled son of a high official.
Iki was trained in Chinese morality and was self-willed and aristocratic.
Iki had planned to make Fukuzawa his life-long follower.
Iki decided that Fukuzawa should be sent home to Nakatsu when he found Fukuzawa flying ahead of him.
Iki was nearly ten years Fukuzawa's senior.
Okudaira Yohei was Iki's father and the old chancellor in Fukuzawa's clan.
Okudaira Yohei was in retirement and called 'Go-Inkyo Sama' with much respect.
Iki urged his father to send an order for Fukuzawa's return to Nakatsu.
Fukuzawa's brother had left for Osaka to assume an official post.
Fukuzawa's mother was living alone at home as all her daughters had married.
Fujimoto Gentai was Fukuzawa's cousin, a doctor and scholar.
Fujimoto Gentai had true sympathy for Fukuzawa's mother.
Okudaira Yohei ordered Fujimoto to write a letter saying Fukuzawa's mother was ill.
Okudaira Yohei told Fujimoto that Yukichi's presence in Nagasaki hampered his son's career.
Fujimoto let Fukuzawa's mother know about the scheme before writing the letter.
Fujimoto explained the transaction to Fukuzawa on a separate sheet and enjoined him to have no anxiety about his mother's health.
Fukuzawa grew very indignant at being commanded to be told a lie.
Fukuzawa wanted at first to challenge Iki in a great argument.
Fukuzawa's better judgment told him it was useless to quarrel with the son of a chancellor.
Fukuzawa went to Iki with a show of surprise and anxiety about his mother's illness.
Iki expressed sympathy and advised Fukuzawa to go home at once.
Iki promised to see that Fukuzawa returned to Nagasaki after his mother's recovery.
Iki gave Fukuzawa an unsealed letter to his father and another to Ohashi Rokusuke.
The unsealed letter said Fukuzawa was being sent home at his own urgent request due to his mother's illness.
The letter instructed that Fukuzawa should be arranged to return to Nagasaki after his mother's recovery.
Fukuzawa opened the unsealed letter at his lodging.

Fukuzawa was impressed by the boldness of Ogata Sensei's conclusions.
Fukuzawa and other students discussed Ogata Sensei's lectures after returning to the dormitory.
Fukuzawa and other students admired Ogata Sensei's learning and ability.
Ogata Sensei was a foremost scholar of Western studies in his time.
Students' street pranks were confined to evenings after reading competitions or days with several days of freedom ahead.
Students worked desperately before reading competitions.
Each student's learning depended upon his own native ability.
Most Ogata students were well skilled in reading foreign texts.
No student at Ogata School was promoted or graduated automatically after years in residence.
Each student had to work his way up by hard labor at Ogata School.
Certain daimyo ordered copies of Doeff's dictionary to be made by Dutch students.
Copying Doeff's dictionary provided a source of income for some students.
One page of Doeff had thirty lines.
Copying the Dutch section of Doeff brought sixteen mon per page.
Copying the Japanese half of Doeff brought eight mon per page.
Copying Doeff paid better than copying ordinary Japanese books.
A complete copy of Doeff's six thousand page dictionary brought a substantial sum.
A koku of rice cost three bu and two shu.
One sho of wine cost between one hundred sixty-four and two hundred mon.
A student's monthly expenses in the dormitory were about one bu and two shu, or less than one hundred mon a day.
A student copying ten pages of Doeff a day could earn more than his cost of living.
Only Dutch students had the privilege of earning their living while studying at a school.
In Yedo, there was much demand for copying various Dutch books.
Wages for copying were higher in Yedo than in Osaka.
Suzuki Giroku from Kanazawa went to Yedo penniless.
Suzuki Giroku earned his living in Yedo by copying Dutch books.
Suzuki Giroku saved about twenty ryo in a year or two.
Suzuki Giroku came to Osaka for more study with his savings.
Suzuki Giroku returned home as an accomplished doctor.
Suzuki Giroku told Fukuzawa that Yedo was good for making a living but not for real study.
There were no examples of industrial machinery in Japan at that time.
A steam engine could not be seen anywhere in Japan at that time.
There was no apparatus for chemical experiments in Japan at that time.
Ogata students learned theories of chemistry and machinery from books.
Ogata students tried to make things illustrated in their books.
Fukuzawa knew from his residence in Nagasaki that iron could be tin-plated with zinc chloride.
In Japan, copper and bronze cooking vessels were tin-plated using pine pitch.
Ogata students decided to plate iron using the modern method.
Standard chloric acid could not be purchased in stores at that time.
Ogata students prepared chloric acid themselves from descriptions in texts.
Ogata students successfully plated iron with tin using their prepared acid.
Tin-plating iron by the modern method was beyond the practice of any tin craftsman in Japan.
Ogata students sought to produce iodine.
Ogata students worked out the chemical formula for iodine together.
Ogata students bought seaweed at the market at Temma.
Ogata students roasted seaweed and applied other processes to produce iodine.
Ogata students were unable to get a satisfactory result in producing iodine.
Ogata students tried to produce ammonium chloride.
Horse-hoof was used as a requisite for the ammonium chloride experiment.
Ogata students obtained horse-hoof fragments from a store selling tortoise-shell ware.
Horse-hoof fragments were quite cheap and could be obtained for the asking.
Ogata students covered horse-hoofs in an earthenware jar with a layer of clay.
Ogata students placed the jar on a charcoal fire in a large bowl.
Ogata students fanned the fire vigorously during the ammonium chloride experiment.
A smelly vapor came out during the ammonium chloride experiment.
The vapor was condensed in an earthenware pipe.
The condensed vapor dripped freely from the pipe initially.
The vapor from the ammonium chloride experiment had an awful stench.
Ogata students' clothing became saturated with the gas from the experiment.
Street dogs howled at Ogata students after the experiment due to the smell.
Ogata students tried the experiment naked to avoid saturating their clothing.
The students' skins absorbed the smell from the experiment.
Young men at Ogata stood the smelly ordeal without complaint.
Neighbors objected to the smell from the ammonium chloride experiment.
Servants in the Ogata household could not eat their meals due to the sickening gas.
The ammonium chloride experiment produced a strange powdery substance.
The powdery substance was not very pure and not the correct crystals of ammonium chloride.
Most young men, including Fukuzawa, decided to stop the ammonium chloride experiment.
Some students were more stouthearted and would not give up the ammonium chloride experiment.
Some students believed giving up unfinished work was a disgrace to their profession.
Some students hired a cheap boat on the Yodo River to continue the ammonium chloride experiment.
Students placed their brazier and utensils on the boat to continue the experiment.
The vapor from the boat experiment penetrated nearby shores.
People came out and yelled at the students to move away.
Students had the boat rowed upstream and downstream to continue the experiment.
Students moved the boat from Tenjin Bridge to Tamae Bridge while continuing the experiment.
Nakamura Kyoan from Kompira in Sanuki province was the chief of the determined students.
Ogata students were interested in chemistry experiments.
Fukuzawa Yukichi and other young men at the Ogata household attempted to manufacture ammonium chloride through chemical experiments.
The chemical experiments at the Ogata household produced noxious gas that made it difficult for residents to eat their meals.
Most young men at the Ogata household, including Fukuzawa, decided to abandon the ammonium chloride experiment after initial hardships.
Some students more determined than others continued pursuing the ammonium chloride experiment by conducting it on a boat in the Yodo River.
Nakamura Kyoan from Kompira in Sanuki province was the chief of the determined students continuing the ammonium chloride experiment.
Ogata students were interested in dissecting animals, stray dogs, cats, and corpses of decapitated criminals.
Fukuzawa describes the Ogata students as a hardened, reckless crowd who were also studious and earnest.
A student named Tsuruta Senan was severely burned when he knocked a bowl of impure sulphuric acid from his shelf onto himself.
The Ogata students ordered wine from a shop called Kometo and kept the earthenware bottles for use in their chemical works.
The wine merchant from Kometo eventually refused to send wine to the Ogata dormitory after learning the bottles were being used for chemical experiments rather than consumed.
Ogata Sensei was a doctor to Lord Kuroda of Chikuzen.
Lord Kuroda passed through Osaka annually on his journey to Yedo, and Ogata would pay his respects at the feudal headquarters on Nakano-shima.
In the third or fourth year of Ansei (1856-1857), Lord Kuroda showed Ogata Sensei a new Dutch translation of a book on physical science.
The book shown by Lord Kuroda contained a full explanation of electricity based on the discoveries of English physicist Faraday, including diagrams of an electric cell.
Fukuzawa was deeply impressed by the chapter on electricity in the Dutch physics book.
Fukuzawa and other Ogata students decided to copy the final chapter on electricity from the borrowed book.
The Ogata students worked day and night in shifts to copy approximately three hundred pages on electricity including diagrams.
The students finished copying the electricity chapter before Lord Kuroda's departure from Osaka.
Lord Kuroda paid eighty ryo for the physics book, a sum that astonished the students.
The copying of the electricity chapter significantly advanced the Ogata students' knowledge of the new science.
Fukuzawa later became a personal friend of Lord Kuroda.
The original physics book was lost after the Restoration of 1868.
Fukuzawa regretted the loss of the original book like separation from an old friend.
Students from Yedo came to study at Ogata's school, but no Ogata students left to study in Yedo.
Fukuzawa and his fellow Osaka students believed they were superior in scholarship to students in Yedo.
In Yedo, there was government and feudal demand for Western knowledge, creating opportunities for language students to become high-salaried samurai.
Osaka was a city of merchants devoted to internal commerce with little demand for Dutch or Western knowledge.
Dutch students in Osaka were looked upon with contempt by most people.
Fukuzawa and his fellow students found pleasure in studying Dutch despite having no clear purpose for their knowledge.
The Ogata students were conscious that they possessed the key to knowledge of European civilization.
Fukuzawa and his fellow students took pride in their knowledge despite their poverty and poor clothing.
The Ogata students rarely discussed political subjects, as most were students of medicine.
The Ogata students were all in favor of free intercourse with Western countries.
The Ogata students constantly attacked Chinese medicine.
Through hating Chinese medicine, the Ogata students came to dislike everything connected with Chinese culture.
The Ogata students believed their country should be rid of Chinese influences altogether.
Fukuzawa and his fellow students felt sorry for young students of Chinese literature.
The Ogata students ridiculed students of Chinese medicine.
A noted doctor of Chinese medicine named Hanaoka had an academy in the Ogata neighborhood.
The students of Hanaoka's academy appeared to be well-to-do and wore better clothing than the Ogata students.
The Ogata and Hanaoka students never exchanged greetings when they met on the streets.
The Ogata students boasted that their Western medicine would eventually replace Chinese medicine.
Most Ogata students were putting all their energy into studies without definite assurance of the future.
Fukuzawa believes that students overly concerned about their future do not get the best results from their education.
In the fifth year of Ansei (1858), when Fukuzawa was twenty-five years old, he left Osaka for Yedo.
Fukuzawa received an order through his clan's headquarters in Yedo to do work there.
Okami Hikozō was an official in Fukuzawa's clan service and an ardent advocate of Dutch culture.
Okami Hikozō had been endeavoring to open a school of Dutch language in his clan's estate in Yedo.
Matsuki Kōan and Sugi Koji were scholars secured to instruct at the Dutch language school.
Okami Hikozō decided to employ Fukuzawa as a teacher rather than scholars from other clans.
Okudaira Iki was the presiding officer of Fukuzawa's clan in Yedo.
Fukuzawa and Okudaira Iki had some hard feelings from the time they both lived in Nagasaki.
Fukuzawa harbored secret resentment toward Okudaira Iki while appearing polite and grateful to him.
Fukuzawa secretly copied one of Okudaira Iki's valuable books.
Okudaira Iki did not object to Fukuzawa being called to Yedo despite being able to prevent it as presiding officer.
Before leaving Osaka for Yedo, Fukuzawa visited his mother.
A great epidemic of cholera was raging at the time of Fukuzawa's visit to his mother.
According to clan rules, a man of Fukuzawa's social rank traveling in official capacity was to have one follower.
Fukuzawa was given expenses for one follower but did not need a serving man.
Okamoto Shukichi, a Hiroshima man, volunteered to accompany Fukuzawa to Yedo.
Okamoto Shukichi later changed his name to Furukawa Setsuzo.
Fukuzawa asked Okamoto to boil rice for him in exchange for taking him to Yedo instead of hiring a servant.
Okamoto agreed to boil rice for Fukuzawa.
Harada Raizō from Bitchu was a third student from the Ogata dormitory leaving for Yedo.
Fukuzawa, Okamoto, and Harada walked three hundred miles from Osaka to Yedo along the old Tokaido highway.
The journey to Yedo took place in late October in good walking weather.
Fukuzawa and his companions were not stopped at any river crossings during their journey.
Upon reaching Yedo, Fukuzawa reported at the Okudaira residence at Kobikicho, Shiodome.
Fukuzawa and Okamoto were assigned quarters in Teppozu where lesser officials had apartments.
Fukuzawa and Okamoto began bachelor's housekeeping in their Teppozu quarters.
Fukuzawa Yukichi traveled from Osaka to Yedo with a companion named Okamoto and another student named Harada Raizō.
The journey from Osaka to Yedo covered three hundred miles along the old Tokaido highway.
The journey took place in late October and was a good season for walking.
Fukuzawa and Okamoto took up lodgings in Teppozu where lesser officials had their apartments.
Harada took up his abode in the household of the great doctor Otsuki Shunsai at Neribei in Shitaya.
Three or four sons and brothers of officials came to Fukuzawa for instruction in Dutch shortly after he was settled.
Five or six men of other clans began to attend Fukuzawa's Dutch instruction.
Fukuzawa took pride in illustrating that students from Osaka came to Yedo to teach, not to study.
Fukuzawa visited Shimamura Teiho, a physician from Ogata's school and an old friend.
Shimamura was engaged in translating foreign books in Yedo.
Shimamura showed Fukuzawa a difficult passage on physiology that he and five or six scholar-friends could not understand.
Fukuzawa was able to translate the difficult passage after studying it silently for half an hour.
The passage dealt with the relation between light and the human eye.
After successfully translating the passage, Fukuzawa secretly decided that few scholars of Dutch in Yedo could surpass him in translation work.
Fukuzawa sometimes took difficult passages to senior scholars to test their ability.
Fukuzawa sought out passages that many students in Ogata's school had mistranslated.
In the sixth year of Ansei (1859), the Treaty of the Five Nations was established and the port of Yokohama was formally opened for trade.
Fukuzawa went to Yokohama for sight-seeing.
When Fukuzawa tried to speak with foreigners in Yokohama, no one seemed to understand him.
Fukuzawa could not understand anything spoken by the foreigners he met.
Fukuzawa could not read the signboards over shops or labels on bottles in Yokohama.
Fukuzawa encountered a German shopkeeper named Kniffer who could understand his Dutch when written.
Fukuzawa had to walk fifty miles round trip to Yokohama and return before midnight when the gate of his compound closed.
Fukuzawa had been striving for many years to learn the Dutch language.
Fukuzawa realized that English was to be the most useful language of the future.
Fukuzawa determined to begin the study of English the day after returning from Yokohama.
There was no teacher of English in Yedo at that time.
Fukuzawa found an interpreter named Moriyama Takichiro who had been called from Nagasaki to help negotiate the new treaty.
Moriyama knew some English though his specialty was Dutch.
Fukuzawa lived in Teppozu and Moriyama's house was in Koishikawa, a distance of about five miles.
Fukuzawa rose early every morning and walked to Moriyama's residence for lessons.
Fukuzawa never had a single lesson with Moriyama due to Moriyama's busy schedule with treaty negotiations.
Moriyama eventually asked Fukuzawa to come in the evening instead of the morning.
Fukuzawa's evening route lay near Kanda Bridge and Hitotsu-bashi, passing through a desolate region called Gojiin-ga Hara full of huge pine trees.
Fukuzawa passed through Gojiin-ga Hara at eleven or twelve at night on his way home.
Fukuzawa went to Moriyama's residence every evening for two or three months without receiving any lessons.
Fukuzawa heard that Moriyama did not really know very much English and only knew the pronunciation of a few words.
Fukuzawa bought two volumes of a small English conversation book at Kniffer's store in Yokohama.
The English conversation book contained sentences in Dutch and English.
No store in Yedo or Yokohama had a foreign dictionary for sale.
Fukuzawa learned that the government school of foreign culture, known as Bansho Shirabesho, had many dictionaries.
Fukuzawa had to enter Bansho Shirabesho as a student to have access to its dictionaries.
Fukuzawa had to get the seal of the highest official of his clan in Yedo on his petition to enter the government school.
Mitsukuri Gempo was the director of Bansho Shirabesho at that time.
Fukuzawa was permitted to enter Bansho Shirabesho as a student.
Fukuzawa could not remove books from Bansho Shirabesho building.
Fukuzawa gave up attending Bansho Shirabesho on the very first day.
Fukuzawa obtained a small Dutch-English dictionary in two volumes called Holtrop that contained phonetic notations.
The Holtrop dictionary cost five ryō.
Fukuzawa petitioned his clan to buy the Holtrop dictionary for him.
Fukuzawa made firm his determination to learn English by his own efforts.
Fukuzawa studied day and night with his new books as sole companions.
Fukuzawa tried to make out English sentences by translating each word into Dutch.
Fukuzawa tried forming English sentences from Dutch vocabulary.
Fukuzawa realized he ought to have a friend to work with him in studying English.
All students of Dutch were facing the keen disappointment of finding that Dutch was not a universal language.
Fukuzawa approached his friend Kanda Kohei about studying English together.
Kanda Kohei confessed he had thought about learning English but found it beyond him and lacked the courage.
Fukuzawa went to Murata Zoroku (later Omura Masujiro) seeking his aid in studying English.
Murata Zoroku believed that all important books would be translated by the Dutch and saw no use in learning English.
Fukuzawa went to Harada Keisaku about studying English.
Harada Keisaku was enthusiastic and promised to join Fukuzawa in studying English.
Fukuzawa and Harada Keisaku began studying English together.
A young lad came to Yedo from Nagasaki who knew some English.
Fukuzawa and Harada tried to get pronunciation from the young lad from Nagasaki.
Ship-wrecked Japanese fishermen were brought back by foreign ships after the new policy of open ports.
Fukuzawa and Harada called on ship-wrecked fishermen at their lodgings to learn English from them.
Pronunciation was the most difficult part of learning English.
The meaning and construction of English sentences was not as difficult as pronunciation.
With the help of a dictionary, replacing every word in an English sentence with a Dutch word made most sentences understandable.
Uneducated fishermen who had been with Englishmen or Americans could serve as teachers for pronunciation.
After initial study, English pronunciation did not prove to be very difficult.
Fukuzawa came to see that English was not as entirely foreign as initially thought.
Dutch and English were both languages of the same origin.
Knowledge of Dutch could be applied directly to English.
In the sixth year of Ansei (1859), the government of the Shogun made a great decision to send a ship-of-war to the United States.
This was an enterprise never before attempted since the foundation of the empire.
Fukuzawa was to have the good fortune of visiting America on this ship.
The ship was a very small sailing craft equipped with an auxiliary steam engine of one hundred horsepower.
The steam engine was used for maneuvering in and out of harbors.
In the open sea the ship must depend entirely on sail.
The government had purchased the ship from the Dutch for 25,000 ryō a few years before.
The ship was named the Kanrin-maru.
Since the second year of Ansei (1855), officers had been studying navigation and the science of steamships under Dutch residents of Nagasaki.
Officers had developed skill and practice that made them able to venture to America.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was settled in Yedo the year before the sixth year of Ansei (1859).
The Shogun's government decided to send a ship-of-war to the United States in the sixth year of Ansei (1859), an enterprise never before attempted since the foundation of the empire.
Fukuzawa Yukichi had the good fortune of visiting America on this ship.
The Kanrin-maru was a very small sailing craft equipped with an auxiliary steam engine of one hundred horsepower.
The government had purchased the Kanrin-maru from the Dutch for 25,000 ryd a few years before.
Since the second year of Ansei (1855), after the opening of the ports, officers had been studying navigation and the science of steamships under the Dutch residents of Nagasaki.
The Kanrin-maru was to act as a kind of escort to the envoy who was sailing on an American warship.
Kimura Settsu-no Kami was the captain of the Kanrin-maru.
Katsu Rintaro was next in command to Captain Kimura.
The entire crew of the Kanrin-maru, including the captain's personal servant, amounted to ninety-six men.
Every member of the crew was determined to take the ship across unassisted by a foreigner.
Captain Brooke was an American officer who had come to Yokohama.
Captain Brooke's ship, the Fenimore Cooper, was wrecked on the southern coast of Japan.
The staff of the Kanrin-maru protested strongly against allowing American navigators to sail with them.
The Kanrin-maru staff's opposition grew out of the sense of honor for themselves and for their country.
Fukuzawa Yukichi's greatest wish was to sail on the voyage to America.
Fukuzawa Yukichi thought Captain Kimura would need personal servants as befitted his rank.
Dr. Katsuragawa was a near relative of Captain Kimura and a physician in service of the Shogun.
Dr. Katsuragawa was looked up to as the patriarch of Dutch learning in Japan by all the students of the country.
Fukuzawa Yukichi had taken the first opportunity of paying his respects to Dr. Katsuragawa when he reached Yedo the year before.
Fukuzawa Yukichi begged Dr. Katsuragawa for a letter of introduction to Captain Kimura.
Fukuzawa Yukichi begged Captain Kimura to take him along as his servant.
Captain Kimura agreed immediately that Fukuzawa Yukichi might join the ship.
Captain Kimura's reason for granting Fukuzawa Yukichi's wish so readily was probably that there were not many volunteers for such an unusual enterprise.
The Kanrin-maru left Yedo from the shores of Shinagawa in January, the first year of Manen (1860).
The envoy was to sail on an American warship, the Powhatan, sent over specially for the official journey of the embassy.
All the officers of the Kanrin-maru were young men.
One of the officers suggested a party to celebrate the ship's leave-taking of the country.
Fukuzawa Yukichi picked up a large china bowl from a restaurant shelf during the leave-taking party.
Fukuzawa Yukichi used the bowl to eat rice and soup while standing up during rough weather at sea.
Fukuzawa Yukichi found the bowl useful on both the voyage to America and the return trip.
Fukuzawa Yukichi later learned that the restaurant where he took the bowl was a place of rendezvous for prostitutes with their customers.
The Kanrin-maru sailed far to the north in winter on rough seas.
The Kanrin-maru lost two of the four life-boats overboard during the voyage.
Fukuzawa Yukichi found the captain's quarters floor covered with dollar coins that had broken loose from bags stored in the closet.
The samurai officials had no knowledge of foreign credit or money-orders at that time.
The samurai officials carried a huge amount of coins in the captain's locker for the voyage.
The ship keeled over at angles of thirty-seven or thirty-eight degrees during storms.
If the ship had gone over forty-five degrees, it would have foundered to the bottom.
For a whole month during the voyage, the crew saw nothing but waves and clouds.
Once the crew sighted a sail boat said to be an American vessel carrying Chinese workmen over to America.
Fukuzawa Yukichi did not suffer from seasickness at all during the voyage.
Fukuzawa Yukichi joked with friends that the voyage was like being in jail with earthquakes day and night.
Fukuzawa Yukichi had no feeling of fear during the voyage.
Fukuzawa Yukichi trusted in Western science through and through.
The supply of water on the ship began to run low during the voyage.
There was a question of making port at the Hawaiian Islands to get more water.
It was finally decided to sail straight on to San Francisco without stopping at the Hawaiian Islands.
An order was issued that no water should be used except for drinking.
The four or five American sailors on the ship were found to be using more water than they were supposed to.
Captain Brooke replied that any seaman found wasting water could be shot, as such a person was guilty of treason to the ship.
The Japanese officers warned the American seamen of the penalty for wasting water.
The crew of ninety-six men reached land at San Francisco after thirty-seven days.
Of the thirty-seven days of the voyage, perhaps four or five had been fine; all the others had been stormy and rainy.
The conditions on board became very bad since the weather made cleaning and drying impossible.
The Japanese sailors wore semi-foreign clothing but had straw sandals on their feet.
There was a supply of hundreds of pairs of straw sandals on board the ship.
In America, by the generosity of the captain, each sailor received a pair of boots.
It was not until the sixth year of Kaei (1853) that a steamship was seen for the first time in Japan.
It was only in the second year of Ansei (1855) that the Japanese began to study navigation from the Dutch in Nagasaki.
By 1860, the science of navigation was sufficiently understood to enable the Japanese to sail a ship across the Pacific.
About seven years after the first sight of a steamship, after only about five years of practice, the Japanese people made a trans-Pacific crossing without help from foreign experts.
The Japanese officers received no aid from Captain Brooke throughout the voyage.
The Japanese officers and the Americans made observations independently of each other during the voyage.
The Japanese were never in the least dependent on the Americans for navigation observations.
Fukuzawa Yukichi felt convinced that there is no other nation in the Orient with the ability or courage to navigate a steamship across the Pacific after a period of five years of experience.
Peter the Great of Russia went to Holland to study navigation.
Peter the Great, with all his attainments, could not have equalled the feat of the Japanese trans-Pacific crossing.
Peter the Great was a man of genius, but his people did not respond to his leadership in the practice of science as did the Japanese.
As soon as the Kanrin-maru came into the port of San Francisco, it was greeted by many important personages who came on board.
Along the shores of San Francisco, thousands of people were lined up to see the strange newcomers.
It had been decided that the Americans on shore should fire a salute.
If the Americans fired a salute, the Kanrin-maru would have to respond with a return salute.
Katsu Rintaro was second in command under Captain Kimura.
Katsu Rintaro proved a very poor sailor and did not leave his cabin during the whole voyage across.
Katsu Rintaro appeared again to take charge of various operations once the ship was in port.
When the question of the salute came up, Katsu demurred and said it would be difficult.
Katsu said that if they did not fire the salute properly, it would bring shame on them.
Sasakura Kiritaro, the First Officer, replied that he could fire the ordnance properly.
Katsu told Sasakura that if he could fire a cannon properly, Katsu would pledge him his head.
Sasakura was roused by Katsu's taunt and swore he would fire off a salute.
Sasakura ordered the sailors to make ready and load the gun.
Sasakura used an hourglass for timing and brought off a salute beautifully.
Sasakura declared that the head of the sub-captain was his, but that it should remain on the man as long as the voyage lasted.
The story of Sasakura's salute made a big story all over the ship.
The American welcome on shore was certainly worthy of a friendly people.
The Americans did everything for the Japanese crew and could not have done more.
The feeling on the Americans' part was like that of a teacher receiving his old pupil several years after graduation.
It was Commodore Perry who had effected the opening of Japan seven years before.
As soon as the crew came on shore, they were driven off in carriages to a hotel.
City officials and various dignitaries came to offer entertainment to the Japanese crew.
The Japanese crew was given quarters in the official residence of the Navy station on Mare Island.
The American hosts arranged that the Japanese food should be prepared by the Japanese crew's own cook.
The American officials sent fish every day to satisfy the Japanese love for seafood.
The American officials had baths prepared daily, knowing the Japanese custom of bathing frequently.
The Kanrin-maru had been damaged by the passing storms and was put in dry dock to be repaired.
The American treatment of the Japanese crew brought to mind an old Japanese expression about a host putting guests on the palm of his hand to see that they lacked nothing.
The Japanese crew had many confusing and embarrassing moments because they were quite ignorant of the customs of American life.
The Japanese crew was surprised even by the carriages.
The Japanese crew did not identify their mode of conveyance as a carriage until the door had been opened, they were seated inside, and the horses had started off.
All of the Japanese crew wore the usual pair of swords at their sides and hemp sandals.
The Japanese crew was taken to a modern hotel while wearing swords and hemp sandals.
Valuable carpets covered the interior of the hotel.
In Japan, valuable carpets could only be bought from importers' shops at so much a square inch to make purses and tobacco pouches.
Fukuzawa Yukichi was part of a mission to America.
The mission members wore swords and hemp sandals.
Fukuzawa was surprised by the use of valuable carpets as floor coverings in American hotels.
Fukuzawa was unfamiliar with champagne and ice.
Fukuzawa did not recognize ash-trays and matches as such.
Fukuzawa accidentally set his sleeve on fire with ashes he thought he had extinguished.
Before arriving in America, Fukuzawa considered himself an independent, care-free student who feared nothing.
Upon arriving in America, Fukuzawa became shy and self-conscious.
Fukuzawa and his companions were bewildered by American dancing.
A Dutch physician lived in Vallejo near Mare Island.
Fukuzawa observed that in the Dutch physician's household, the mistress entertained guests while the doctor directed servants, which was the reverse of Japanese domestic custom.
Fukuzawa was shocked to see a whole roasted pig served at dinner.
Captain Kimura was an accomplished horseman who used to ride daily in Yedo.
Americans praised the Japanese ability in riding.
Fukuzawa had studied scientific principles at Ogata's school.
Fukuzawa already knew the principles of telegraphy before seeing the actual machine.
Fukuzawa knew that sugar was bleached by straining with bone-black.
Fukuzawa was surprised by the enormous waste of iron in America.
Fukuzawa was surprised by the high cost of daily commodities in California.
Fukuzawa paid a half-dollar for a bottle of oysters containing only twenty or thirty oysters.
Fukuzawa asked an American gentleman about the descendants of George Washington.
The American gentleman replied casually that he thought there was a woman descended from Washington who was married.
Fukuzawa expected the Washington family to be revered above all other families.
Fukuzawa's reasoning was based on the reverence in Japan for founders of great lines of rulers like Ieyasu of the Tokugawa family.
Captain McDougall was a collector of coins.
Captain Kimura had Japanese coins arranged in sequence to show Captain McDougall.
Captain McDougall's wife brought flowers to Captain Kimura to thank him for the gift of coins.
Fukuzawa was moved by the American lady's act of thanking for gold and silver with flowers.
The Americans repaired the damaged ship without charging for the repairs.
The Americans built lockers on the ship for the crew's use.
Fukuzawa and the interpreter Nakahama each bought a copy of Webster's dictionary.
This was the first importation of Webster's dictionary into Japan.
Fukuzawa felt no disappointment about leaving America after securing Webster's dictionary.
Eight years after the first mission, Fukuzawa met Captain Brooke again in America.
Captain Brooke wanted the welcome to be a gay festive event.
Army officers in San Francisco said they could not arrange a military escort without permission from Washington.
Captain Brooke went to the San Francisco militia instead.
The San Francisco militia was composed of volunteers.
The militia commander was a doctor and the lieutenant was a dyeing business proprietor.
The militia was well equipped with uniforms and rifles.
The militia drilled on holidays and moonlight nights.
Captain Brooke told Fukuzawa as a joke how much the welcoming of Japan's warship meant for the young militiamen.
The ship sailed out of San Francisco with a naval salute.
The ship made a call at the Hawaiian Islands on the return voyage.
